data.title: United Airlines recovers as CEO calms Newark concerns
data.description:
United Airlines (NASDAQ:UAL) has recovered early losses and is clinging to a modest gain on Thursday, as the company’s underwhelming second quarter results were offset by assurances from the CEO regarding the second half of this year and disruptions at a major hub.

“By the fourth quarter [of 2025], there will be no impact on United from Newark airport,” United chief executive Scott Kirby said in an interview on CNBC this morning.

The carrier was forced to cancel 35 daily roundtrip flights out of Newark Liberty International Airport beginning in May as a shortage of air traffic controllers, antiquated technology, and a shuttered runway caused significant disruptions at one of the three major airports serving the New York metropolitan area.

"It's disappointing to make further cuts to an already reduced schedule at Newark, but since there is no way to resolve the near-term structural FAA staffing issues, we feel like there is no other choice in order to protect our customers,” Kirby said in early May.

“We feel better about Newark,” Kirby said on Thursday, acknowledging steps the FAA took to “put Newark on the right footing” by matching the number of flights to the capacity of the airport.

While United’s Newark problem may be in the rearview mirror, the carrier struck a cautious tone regarding Q3 and full year guidance given the lingering softness in early 2025 travel demand. However, over the past three weeks, demand has picked up considerably, which will likely make FY25 guidance look too conservative.

The company expects to earn a profit of $9 to $11 per share, which, at the midpoint, is slightly below the consensus estimates.

“We believe the street will look at forward guide as largely consistent with expectations reflecting a choppy macro and idiosyncratic [Newark] issues,” Morgan Stanley’s Ravi Shanker said in a note to clients, while Seeking Alpha analyst Dilantha De Silva views the guidance as a “prudent move that establishes an achievable earnings floor, making the outlook more reliable than before.”

Coupled with Delta’s (DAL) upbeat outlook, the airline sector is enjoying a renewed confidence, reflected in a 15% gain in the U.S. Global Jets ETF (JETS) over the past 30 days (versus 5% for the S&P 500).

With Newark disruptions fading and demand showing signs of recovery, United's cautious guidance may ultimately set the stage for upside surprises later in the year.

“Despite the headwinds we faced in the first half of the year, we delivered significant growth in EPS, and we feel very good about the core fundamentals of our airline in the second half of 2025 and beyond,” Kirby concluded on the call.

The uplift in United (NASDAQ:UAL) is giving a boost to the sector with Delta Air Lines (DAL) up 1%, American Airlines (AAL) up 1.3%, and JetBlue (JBLU) shares up 1.4%.

data.title: Webster Financial outlines $1B–$2.5B HSA deposit growth outlook as regulatory changes expand market
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Webster Financial Corporation (WBS) Q2 2025

Management View

  • CEO John R. Ciulla reported "our results were solid with a return on tangible common equity of 18%, ROAA of nearly 1.3% and growth in both loans and deposits of over 1% linked quarter." He highlighted revenue growth of 1.6% over the prior quarter and an increase in the common equity Tier 1 ratio. The Board authorized an additional $700 million in share repurchases, with 1.5 million shares bought back in the quarter.
  • Ciulla stated, "The inflection point in asset quality that we projected to occur in mid-2025 is materializing. Both criticized commercial loans and non-accruals were down in the quarter." He also noted no new pockets of credit deterioration and minimal exposure to industries potentially affected by tariffs.
  • The CEO emphasized strategic advantages in the Healthcare Financial Services segment, calling HSA Bank and Ametros "a growing source of low-cost, long duration and very sticky deposits." He underscored provisions in the reconciliation bill that "should also accelerate growth in HSA deposits."
  • Ciulla announced, "The asset management partnership with Marathon we announced last year will be effective as of later today, and we believe that will enhance sponsor loan growth and drive fee revenue in 2026 and beyond."
  • President and COO Luis R. Massiani highlighted favorable regulatory changes: "We estimate the potential deposit opportunity for HSA Bank over the next 5 years ranges from $1 billion to $2.5 billion of additional deposits, starting with incremental growth next year of $50 million to $100 million."
  • Massiani explained the ramp-up in the private credit joint venture with Marathon, stating, "We moved $242 million of loans in held-for-sale status as these loans will be contributed to the joint venture, which we expect will be up and running in the third quarter."
  • CFO William Holland summarized, "Total assets were $82 billion at period end, up $1.6 billion from last quarter with growth in loans, cash and securities. Deposits were up over $700 million." Holland noted net income to common shareholders rose $31 million compared to the prior quarter and EPS was $1.52 versus $1.30 in the first quarter.
  • Ciulla welcomed Jason Schugel as the new Chief Risk Officer and announced Fred Crawford as a new Board member.

Outlook

  • Management now expects full year 2025 NII of $2.47 billion to $2.5 billion. Holland stated, "We now expect NII of $2.47 billion to $2.5 billion on a non-FTE basis. This assumes 2 Fed funds rate cuts beginning in September."
  • The full year tax rate is forecasted in the range of 20% to 21%. Holland added, "Year-to-date, we are at a 20% effective tax rate due to discrete benefits, but we expect the rate to return to 21% in the second half of the year."
  • Holland affirmed, "We are still expecting a net interest margin of approximately 3.4% this year," anticipating a year-end exit between 3.35% and 3.40%.

Financial Results

  • Total assets reached $82 billion, with loan growth of $616 million (1.2% linked quarter) and deposit growth of $739 million. Excluding the $242 million onetime transfer to held-for-sale, loan growth would have been $858 million (1.6%).
  • EPS was $1.52, up from $1.30 in the first quarter. Tangible book value per common share increased to $35.13 from $33.97.
  • Net interest income rose by $9 million over Q1, and non-interest income increased by $2.1 million. Expenses increased by $2 million, with an efficiency ratio of 45.4%.
  • Provision for loan losses was $47 million, down from the prior quarter, and allowance for loan losses increased to $722 million or 1.35% of loans.

Q&A

  • Andrew Steven Leischner, KBW: Asked about capital targets and buybacks. Ciulla replied, "I would still say that for the balance of '25, that 11% target is probably the right amount...over time that we can reduce the level comfortably and safely of our CET1 ratio."
  • Casey Haire, Autonomous Research: Inquired about NIM outlook and cash balances. Ciulla responded, "In this quarter, building cash had a 1 basis point impact to NIM. We expect an additional 1 basis points throughout the rest of this year over the next 2 quarters."
  • Mark Thomas Fitzgibbon, Piper Sandler: Questioned deposit costs and potential for bank M&A. Ciulla explained, "If we get additional cuts, we expect to continue to move our deposit costs down. If we don't...we don't see material opportunity to continue to move down deposit costs."
  • Jared Shaw, Barclays: Asked about HSA direct-to-consumer channel and expenses. Massiani answered, "No material change in the expense trajectory of HSA...there will be some spend on the marketing and education front."
  • Matthew M. Breese, Stephens: Discussed C&I and CRE originations. Holland noted, "There was a little bit of pent-up demand...the pipeline of activity in both commercial -- C&I and commercial real estate has gotten better over the course of May and June."
  • Anthony Albert Elian, JPMorgan: Queried about credit quality. Ciulla stated, "Our risk rating migration has really stabilized, and we're not seeing any new pockets of problems."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts pressed on capital deployment, margin outlook, deposit costs, credit quality, and the scalability of new ventures. The tone was neutral to slightly positive, with some cautious probing on sustainability of growth and risk exposure.
  • Management maintained a confident and measured tone. Ciulla used phrases such as "we are comfortable," "our proactive approach," and "we feel good about the competitive positioning." During Q&A, management provided detailed responses, often reiterating previous guidance and emphasizing flexibility and optionality.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, both analysts and management expressed more confidence in credit stabilization and growth prospects, with management explicitly noting an inflection point in asset quality.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance for NII and tax rate was refined, with NII now expected at $2.47 billion to $2.5 billion and tax rate guidance at 20–21%, compared to the previous quarter's unchanged outlook.
  • Asset quality has improved, with declines in criticized and non-accrual loans, contrasting with the prior quarter's increases.
  • The HSA deposit opportunity and Marathon joint venture have progressed to more concrete implementation phases, with explicit deposit growth figures and operational milestones.
  • Management's tone shifted from cautious optimism to a more confident outlook, especially regarding credit and strategic initiatives, while analysts shifted from skepticism over credit quality to interest in capital allocation and business expansion.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management noted ongoing vigilance regarding potential impacts from tariffs but reported no disproportionate exposure.
  • Competitive deposit environment and deposit cost pressures were highlighted, with continued monitoring by management.
  • Analysts questioned sustainability of loan growth and margin pressures, and sought clarity on risk management regarding specific asset classes such as rent-regulated multifamily portfolios.

Final Takeaway

Webster Financial's second quarter demonstrated solid loan and deposit growth, improving asset quality, and the successful advancement of strategic initiatives in healthcare and asset management. Management emphasized the positive impact of regulatory changes on HSA Bank's potential deposit growth, the operational launch of the Marathon partnership, and disciplined capital deployment. The company's strong capital position, enhanced credit quality, and diversified business lines position it to capitalize on emerging opportunities and maintain resilience in a competitive banking environment.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Banner Corporation signals stable loan growth target and margin tailwinds as core deposits hold at 89%
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Banner Corporation (BANR) Q2 2025

Management View

  • Mark J. Grescovich, President and CEO, reported "Banner Corporation reported a net profit available to common shareholders of $45.5 million or $1.31 per diluted share for the quarter ended June 30, 2025." He highlighted that "our strategy to maintain a moderate risk profile and the investments we have made and continue to make in order to improve operating performance have positioned the company well for the future." Core earnings for Q2 2025 reached $62 million, with core operations revenue at $163 million. Grescovich stated the company's "core deposits continue to represent 89% of total deposits," and noted core deposit and loan growth of 4% and 5% year-over-year, respectively. A dividend of $0.48 per share was announced. Banner continues to receive recognition for its performance, including being named one of America's 100 Best Banks and receiving high marks from Forbes, Newsweek, and J.D. Power & Associates.
  • Jill M. Rice, Chief Credit Officer, noted "loan originations increased 80% when compared to the linked quarter, with commercial real estate up 484%, C&I originations up 96% and construction and land development increasing 43%, respectively." She described loan outstandings growing by $252 million in the quarter, or 9% on an annualized basis, and credit metrics remaining stable with delinquent loans at 0.41% of total loans. Rice warned, "if [trade] policy changes again being suggested as imminent are adopted, they will almost certainly have a negative impact on the West Coast economies."
  • Robert G. Butterfield, CFO, stated "we reported $1.31 per diluted share for the second quarter compared to $1.30 per diluted share for the prior quarter." He explained the increase was "primarily due to an increase in net interest income partially offset by the current quarter, including costs associated with consolidating back office space as well as a higher provision for credit losses due to growth in the loan balances." Butterfield emphasized "Banner's liquidity and capital profile continue to remain strong with robust core funding base, a low reliance on wholesale borrowing and significant off-balance sheet borrowing capacity."

Outlook

  • Jill Rice projected, "I'm still expecting us to hit that mid-single-digit growth rate for the year. I expect we'll see a little bit of a pullback in Q3 but we had a 5% annualized year-over-year in Q1. We had a 5% annualized year-over-year in Q2, and that's roughly what we're projecting for the year of 2025."
  • Butterfield provided, "If normal seasonality returns, we would expect that we would see deposit growth happen in the third quarter and deposit growth could very well outpace loan growth in the third quarter if historical kind of trends come in line."
  • No explicit comparison to analysts' estimates was provided due to lack of data.

Financial Results

  • Banner reported $1.31 per diluted share for Q2 2025, a slight increase from the prior quarter. Core earnings were $62 million and revenue from core operations was $163 million.
  • The loan-to-deposit ratio ended the quarter at 87%. Net interest income increased by $3.3 million from the prior quarter. The tax equivalent net interest margin was 3.92%, unchanged from Q1 2025. Earning asset yields increased 5 basis points, while funding costs also rose by 5 basis points. Deposit costs held at 1.47%.
  • Total loans increased $265 million during the quarter, with core deposit levels stable at 89% of total deposits. Noninterest income decreased by $1.4 million, affected by asset disposal and fair value adjustments. Noninterest expenses remained similar to the prior quarter, with $834,000 in back office consolidation costs included.

Q&A

  • David Pipkin Feaster, Raymond James: Asked about the drivers behind strong loan originations and whether it signals increased business confidence. Rice answered, "the increase in origination certainly pulled some of the pipeline out until they're rebuilding now...I'm still expecting us to hit that mid-single-digit growth rate for the year."
  • Feaster: Inquired about deposit competition and funding for loan growth. Butterfield responded, "We're not necessarily seeing competition heat up on deposits at this point...the ultra competitive environment that we experienced really a year ago is not quite what we're seeing right now."
  • Feaster: Asked about use of FHLB advances to bridge funding gaps. Butterfield explained, "Yes, it was a plug for this quarter certainly...If normal seasonality returns, we would expect that we would see deposit growth happen in the third quarter and deposit growth could very well outpace loan growth."
  • Robert Andrew Terrell, Stephens: Sought details on sub debt redemption and expense outlook. Butterfield detailed, "The cost on that at the time...was 5%...we would expect some pick-up reduction in funding costs...we continue to go live with some of the different modules on the new deposit and loan origination system."
  • Terrell: Inquired about M&A. Grescovich stated, "opportunistic M&A is something we will continue to look at. But I don't feel compelled that we have to do anything."
  • Jeffrey Allen Rulis, D.A. Davidson: Clarified the loan growth outlook for Q3. Rice responded, "It's a pullback from the 9% growth rate...third quarter is generally a little slower than second quarter."
  • Rulis: Asked about margin outlook. Butterfield said, "As long as the Fed is on pause...we would expect loan yields to continue to increase 4 to 5 basis points in the quarter."
  • Rulis: Asked about credit risks. Rice highlighted, "The agricultural sector has experienced more downgrades due to the pressure on commodities prices and input costs."
  • Kelly Ann Motta, KBW: Asked about loan pricing and spreads. Rice shared, "pricing on the term pieces...there hasn't been a lot of change...the yields are holding up."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts focused on sustainability of loan growth, margin outlook, funding strategy, and credit quality, with a neutral to slightly positive tone and multiple questions on operating trends and risk management.
  • Management maintained a confident tone in prepared remarks, using phrases such as "our strategy to maintain a moderate risk profile...have positioned the company well for the future," but acknowledged headwinds with caution regarding tariffs and regional economic risks. The tone remained slightly positive in Q&A, with clear explanations and proactive responses.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management's confidence around credit and funding was steady, with slightly more caution expressed about potential policy changes.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Net profit per share increased slightly from $1.30 in Q1 2025 to $1.31 in Q2 2025.
  • Core earnings rose from $59 million to $62 million, and revenue from core operations grew from $160 million to $163 million.
  • Loan growth accelerated (originations up 80% linked quarter), while credit metrics modestly improved with delinquent loans declining from 0.63% to 0.41% of total loans.
  • Deposit and funding conditions remained stable, with core deposits at 89% of total deposits in both quarters.
  • Management tone remained confident but with heightened caution regarding future policy changes and their potential impact.
  • Analysts continued to focus on margin, loan growth, and credit quality, with consistent themes around funding and risk.

Risks and Concerns

  • Rice reiterated, "if [trade] policy changes again being suggested as imminent are adopted, they will almost certainly have a negative impact on the West Coast economies with the majority of the burden borne by the small business sector and further stressing the consumer."
  • Credit quality in the agricultural sector remains a concern, with pressure from commodity prices and input costs.
  • Nonperforming assets remained modest but were monitored, with most nonperforming loans in consumer-related residential mortgages requiring longer resolution.
  • Management emphasized the need to watch for negative implications from potential new tariffs or policy changes.

Final Takeaway

Banner Corporation's Q2 2025 results highlighted continued organic growth, strong deposit retention, and robust core earnings, underpinned by stable credit quality and a moderate risk profile. Management projects maintaining a mid-single-digit loan growth rate for the year and expects margin tailwinds to persist in the near term, provided interest rates remain stable. While confidence in the business model remains high, the company remains vigilant regarding the potential impact of new trade policies, especially on small businesses and the agricultural sector. Investors are likely to monitor Banner's ability to sustain growth and manage emerging risks as market conditions evolve.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Meta's Threads app to be headed by longtime executive Connor Hayes
data.description:
Connor Hayes has been chosen to helm Meta Platforms' (NASDAQ:META) text-based social media app Threads, Axios reported on Thursday.

Hayes, a longtime company employee, will take over from Instagram head Adam Mosseri, who was overseeing Threads.

"Given Threads' maturity, we think we need a dedicated app lead who can focus all of their time on helping Threads move forward," Mosseri reportedly wrote in a staff memo, which was seen by Axios.

The microblogging app has gained over 380M users as of July 2025, still below its direct rival X's user base of 600M.

Hayes joined Meta in 2011 and was a key member of the team that built the app to compete directly with the platform formerly known as Twitter. He later served as the company's vice president of product for generative AI.

Hayes will assume the new role by mid-September and will report directly to Mosseri.

data.title: ManpowerGroup projects Q3 2025 EPS of $0.77–$0.87 amid stabilization and AI-driven strategy
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: ManpowerGroup (MAN) Q2 2025

Management View

  • Jonas Prising, Chairman & CEO, opened the call stating that "employers facing not only macroeconomic complexity, but also continued geopolitical tensions are proving resilient." He highlighted that the global hiring outlook is "holding steady, up very slightly year-over-year and just 1 point lower than last quarter." Prising noted stabilization in the U.S. and parts of Europe, along with a return to revenue growth in the Manpower and Talent Solutions brands.

  • Prising reported system-wide revenue of $4.9 billion and stated, "Reported revenue was $4.5 billion, down 3% year-over-year in constant currency." He emphasized the company's ability to leverage proprietary data for real-time market assessment, citing "solid momentum in consumer goods across both the U.S. and Europe alongside encouraging signals in aerospace and defense."

  • Prising described targeted actions to optimize performance, especially in sectors like automotive facing headwinds, and said, "We continue to build a strong enterprise sales pipeline, simplify our organization and manage costs with discipline by prioritizing growth initiatives that will deliver the greatest value."

  • John Thomas McGinnis, Executive VP, CFO, stated, "U.S. dollar reported revenues in the second quarter were impacted by foreign currency translation and after adjusting for currency impacts, came in at the high end of our constant currency guidance range." He added, "Gross profit margin came in just below the low end of our guidance range, driven by shifts within staffing, reflecting an increased mix of enterprise accounts."

Outlook

  • McGinnis provided third quarter guidance, forecasting "earnings per share for the third quarter to be in the range of $0.77 to $0.87." He expects a "constant currency revenue guidance range... between a flat and 4% decrease and at the midpoint is a 2% decrease." The guidance includes a favorable foreign currency impact of $0.03 per share, and he projects EBITDA margin "to be down 50 basis points at the midpoint compared to the prior year."

  • Management anticipates stability in most markets and continued existing trends into Q3 2025. They estimate the effective tax rate for the full year on an adjusted basis at 46.5%, with Q3 slightly higher at 48% due to a previously disclosed French tax change.

Financial Results

  • Prising reported a system-wide revenue of $4.9 billion and a reported revenue of $4.5 billion for the quarter. Reported EBITDA was $72 million, while adjusted EBITDA was $89 million, a 25% decrease in constant currency year-over-year. Reported EBITDA margin was 1.6%, and adjusted EBITDA margin was 2.0%.

  • Reported earnings per basic share was (-$1.44), while adjusted earnings per diluted share was $0.78, representing a 43% decrease year-over-year in constant currency. Restructuring costs and disposition losses totaled $0.43 per share, and noncash goodwill and intangible impairment charges were $1.79 per share.

  • Manpower brand achieved 1% constant currency growth, Experis declined by 9%, and Talent Solutions grew by 1%. Gross margin settled at 16.9%. By business line, Manpower comprised 62% of gross profit, Experis 22%, and Talent Solutions 16%. SG&A expenses as adjusted were down 3% year-over-year in constant currency.

  • Americas segment revenue rose to $1.1 billion, Italy revenue increased 4% to $476 million, and Japan revenue grew 7% in constant currency. Free cash flow saw an outflow of $207 million, with the company repurchasing 230,000 shares for $12 million during the quarter.

Q&A

  • Ryan, BMO Capital Markets, asked about market share gains and franchise model expansion. Prising responded that AI is "augmenting human capabilities" and enabling more precise sales targeting, leading to "50% higher revenue than when we do this through human intervention only." On further franchise moves, Prising noted ongoing evaluation of markets where local approaches could be beneficial.

  • Princy Thomas, Barclays, inquired about U.S. trends and seasonality. McGinnis explained, "the U.S. business came in at minus 3% for the quarter, actually slightly better than what we expected when we gave the guide." He highlighted strong Manpower brand performance at "plus 9% in the quarter." McGinnis noted seasonality with typically improved margin in Q3 and anticipated strong free cash flow in the second half.

  • Mark Marcon, Baird, questioned Northern Europe's revenue and profitability outlook. Prising stated, "this continues to be areas where demand eventually will come back" and attributed challenges to economic and geopolitical headwinds. McGinnis added, "We do expect to start to see an improvement in that profitability trend as we go forward based on the savings we're going to be getting from those restructuring actions."

  • Andy Grobler, BNP, asked about fiscal spending in Germany and back-office transformation. Prising said clients in Germany are "optimistic on the outlook yet waiting to see these increased funds... not really going to see any material change during 2025." McGinnis confirmed, "We are tracking very well" on transformation, with "almost 65% of our revenues going through that platform."

  • Further questions touched on AI impacts, the clerical business, perm activity trends, cash flow, and the dividend policy, with management emphasizing ongoing transformation, stabilization, and readiness for future growth.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts' tone was neutral to slightly positive, focusing on market share strategy, cost actions, regional performance, and technology transformation, with some concern about persistent headwinds in Northern Europe and the magnitude of cash outflows.

  • Management maintained a confident and pragmatic tone during both prepared remarks and Q&A, frequently emphasizing stabilization, resilience, and targeted strategic actions. Phrases such as "we are very pleased with the outcomes" and "we are confident..." were used, and while management acknowledged challenges, they highlighted progress and opportunities, especially regarding AI and cost management. Compared to the previous quarter, management's tone shifted from caution to cautious optimism, particularly around stabilization and growth prospects.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance language shifted from a cautious Q2 outlook to a more stable and slightly optimistic Q3 outlook, with management now projecting flat organic revenue trends at the midpoint for Q3.

  • Strategic focus has intensified on AI-driven sales targeting and operational transformation, as well as expanding the franchise model.

  • Key metric changes include a move from Q1's $4.1 billion revenue, $0.44 adjusted EPS, and 1.3% adjusted EBITDA margin to Q2's $4.5 billion revenue, $0.78 adjusted EPS, and 2.0% adjusted EBITDA margin. However, adjusted EPS and EBITDA margin remain significantly lower year-over-year.

  • Analysts and management both displayed increased confidence this quarter, with less concern about immediate risks from macroeconomic uncertainty compared to Q1.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited ongoing economic and geopolitical headwinds, particularly in Northern Europe, and cited the impact of restructuring charges and impairment in Switzerland and the U.K.

  • Cash flow remains a concern due to timing of payables, large tax and technology prepayments, and MSP program payments, with expectations for improvement in the second half.

  • Analysts highlighted risks related to persistent softness in certain markets and the pace of recovery in Europe, as well as questions about permanent placement trends and the dividend policy.

Final Takeaway

Management emphasized a return to revenue growth in key brands, stabilization in major markets, and continued investment in digital transformation and AI capabilities. While macroeconomic uncertainty and regional headwinds persist, the company expects ongoing stability and slight improvement into Q3, supported by robust enterprise pipelines, targeted cost controls, and innovation in workforce solutions. The outlook for Q3 2025 reflects cautious optimism, with a projected EPS range of $0.77 to $0.87 and continued strategic focus on profitable growth and technological advancement.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Triumph Financial outlines intelligence segment as fastest-growing transportation business with 20% annual aggregate growth target
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Triumph Financial (TFIN) Q2 2025

Management View

  • Aaron P. Graft, CEO, opened the call by addressing a “noisy quarter's results, but underneath that, albeit positive distraction, laid out a quarter with a lot going in the right direction, particularly in our transportation businesses and around revenue growth.” He highlighted the resolution of the five-year dispute with the United States Postal Service, stating, “Beyond the financial recoupment of all that we were owed, I hope investors can see that as just 1 piece of evidence that you can take Triumph that it's worked.” Graft emphasized core transportation business revenue growth and material credit quality improvement, and urged investors to review the value chain section in the shareholder letter for a deeper strategic understanding. He also welcomed Dawn Favier as President of Intelligence.
  • Graft discussed the Greenscreens acquisition, revealing the business had “roughly $10 million in contracted ARR” and that integration of Triumph’s data would “make their models better, more precise and give them broader coverage.” He asserted that of their three transportation businesses—factoring, payments, intelligence—“intelligence as a percentage will grow the fastest in the next 2 to 3 years.”
  • Dawn Favier reported early post-acquisition traction, with average contract value (ACV) in the pipeline rising from $37,000 pre-deal to $80,000, and significant improvements in lane density and model accuracy due to Triumph’s data.
  • Todd N. Ritterbusch, President, stated, “I expect EBITDA margin to continue to improve as we continue to scale revenues without scaling expenses as fast. My long-term goal is to get us above 40%.”
  • CFO William Bradley Voss commented, “There's probably about a little over $2.2 million or so from green screen specifically that we've added to our run rate. And then that $1.8 million of ongoing amortization. So that contributes about $4 million to our quarterly expense run rate of that $104 million that we talked about going forward.”

Outlook

  • Graft reiterated an expectation that the intelligence segment will “grow more quickly than any of our transportation-related businesses.” He stated, “I will be disappointed if our transportation businesses in the aggregate don't grow at 20% a year, because we have invested heavily to build the opportunity to create revenue growth there.” Management also indicated that Greenscreens integration is expected to drive accelerated revenue growth and that repricing conversations in the payments segment will accelerate, especially with larger brokers in the coming quarters.
  • There was no explicit numerical EPS or revenue guidance provided for the next quarter or year in the transcript.

Financial Results

  • Graft highlighted a positive quarter for the core transportation business, with revenue growth and improved credit quality. He pointed out that the impact from resolving the USPS dispute contributed to a “noisy” quarter, but was not the only driver of results.
  • CFO Voss clarified that the $1.2 million on the USPS fee collection “was all in interest income.”
  • The company reported that the payments segment’s EBITDA margin was “closer to 14%” for the quarter, with expectations for continued improvement.
  • On credit, Graft shared, “If you normalize for those 2 things, our charge-offs were less than $1 million. So an extremely good quarter from a credit perspective.”

Q&A

  • Matthew Covington Olney, Stephens: Asked about Greenscreens integration and customer adoption. Graft explained the three phases: existing Greenscreens, integration with Triumph’s $40 billion of audit and payment data, and the upcoming Triumph intelligence product. Favier added that ACV in the pipeline is up to $80,000, and data ingestion, though less than 5% complete, is already improving model accuracy.
  • Olney further inquired about payment segment EBITDA margins. Ritterbusch: “I expect EBITDA margin to continue to improve as we continue to scale revenues without scaling expenses as fast.”
  • Olney also asked about seasonality in the intelligence segment. Favier: “No, there really is no seasonality associated with that business... we had our largest growth in a down market period. So that business is really stable from a seasonality and volatility perspective.”
  • Joseph Peter Yanchunis, Raymond James: On LoadPay average revenue per account. Ritterbusch replied that mature, seasoned accounts are already generating “$700 or more on average and in some cases, significantly higher.”
  • Tim Switzer, KBW: On Greenscreens’ financials and break-even timeline. Voss confirmed the expense and revenue run rates, and Graft outlined a $3 million quarterly earnings drag from Greenscreens, expecting intelligence to “grow into that earnings drag over the next several quarters.”
  • Gary Peter Tenner, D.A. Davidson: Asked about competitive impacts from DAT’s acquisition. Graft responded that competition is a fundamental part of capitalism and that “that will just be 1 of about 400 factoring companies that we compete with.”

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts’ questions focused on growth drivers, integration progress, competitive risks, and monetization, reflecting a tone of cautious optimism paired with probing on execution and revenue acceleration.
  • Management’s tone, both in prepared remarks and responses, was confident and strategic. Graft repeatedly emphasized deliberate execution and value creation, using phrases like “we will work deliberately to deliver on our promises” and “I will be disappointed if our transportation businesses in the aggregate don't grow at 20% a year.”
  • Compared to the previous quarter, analysts shifted from focusing on overcoming freight headwinds to seeking details on integration, competitive positioning, and near-term financial impact, while management’s tone shifted from defensive to more assertively growth-oriented.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Current quarter featured the successful resolution of the USPS dispute and the close of the Greenscreens acquisition, compared to last quarter’s focus on foundational investments and positioning for growth.
  • Strategic focus shifted from weathering freight market headwinds to integrating acquisitions and ramping up monetization of core products.
  • Guidance language turned more forward-looking, with explicit targets for intelligence segment growth and aggregate transportation business targets.
  • Analysts’ questions evolved from revenue split timing and segment monetization to deeper questions on new business integration, competitive threats, and margin trajectory.
  • Management’s confidence increased, with less emphasis on defensive positioning and more on capturing growth opportunities and leveraging scale.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management acknowledged the earnings drag from the Greenscreens acquisition, with Graft stating, “the impact on near-term earnings is like, call it, $3 million a quarter of drag.”
  • The company is investing significantly in information security and infrastructure to support volume growth and defend against fraud, as Graft explained, “we just have to be bulletproof. But we are not ignorant of it. It is a fair question. And what we owe you frankly... is we said we are going to hold expenses flat from here while we grow revenue.”
  • Analysts raised concerns about competitive entrants, particularly DAT’s factoring move, and the sustainability of account revenue and margins as new products scale.

Final Takeaway

Triumph Financial’s Q2 2025 call emphasized the acceleration of its intelligence segment as the fastest-growing area within its transportation businesses, supported by the Greenscreens integration and leveraging proprietary data. Management set a clear expectation for 20% annual aggregate growth in transportation, continued improvements in payment margins, and disciplined investment in security and infrastructure to support scaling. Despite short-term earnings impact from acquisitions, the company is focused on driving long-term revenue growth and margin expansion while managing risks from competition and operational complexity.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: GSK falls as FDA advisors vote against approving blood cancer drug
data.description:
• A group of independent advisors to the U.S. Food and Drug Administration (FDA) on Thursday voted against the approval of a combination drug regimen involving GSK’s (NYSE:GSK) blood cancer medicine Blenrep.
  • The FDA’s Oncologic Drugs Advisory Committee (ODAC) voted 5-3 against the benefit-risk profile of Blenrep in combination with bortezomib and dexamethasone for relapsed/refractory multiple myeloma as a late-line therapy.
  • The panelists voted 7-1 against the drug’s benefit-risk profile with pomalidomide and dexamethasone for the same indication.
  • The British pharmaceutical company's Biologics License Application targeted at getting U.S. approval for the combination drug for R/R multiple myeloma as a late-line therapy is currently under FDA review with a target action date of July 23.
  • The FDA's advisory committees issue non-binding recommendations. However, the regulator generally follows them before making a final decision on authorizations.

data.title: SF Fed's Daly expects the neutral rate to settle higher than the pre-pandemic level
data.description:
"Right now, when I look at the economy and (monetary) policy, I think they're in a good place," San Francisco Fed President Mary Daly said Thursday at the 2025 Rocky Mountain Economic Summit.

Whether the next rate cut comes in July or September isn't important, she said. What's relevant is "the rate travel," or the direction and pace of movement.

Deciding on the next rate cut hinges on the labor market as well as progress being made in lowering inflation. "In all likelihood," she expects interest rates to settle at a higher rate than they were before the pandemic.

Daly expects that the neutral rate — or the point at which interest rates neither boost nor slow economic growth — is at 3% or north of 3%.

The economy is responding to the Federal Reserve's monetary policy, and inflation is moving down toward, but hasn't yet reached, the central bank's 2% goal. "We're in a good place, and we need to finish the job," she said.

The Fed started raising its policy rate aggressively in 2022 in an effort to tame inflation that flared up after the pandemic. The federal funds rate target range now stands at 4.25%-4.50%.

"Price stability is when people don't have to worry about inflation," Daly said. She described inflation as "an unpredictable tax."

The impacts from tariffs on the economy can develop along three scenarios. 

Businesses' first impulse is to raise prices to consumers to deal with tariffs. However, there's the recognition that consumers are sensitive to prices after post-pandemic inflation surge.

She hasn't seen any evidence that tariffs are leading to persistent inflation, so far. Another scenario is a one-off price increase. 

"They're unlikely to pass the whole thing through," she said regarding the rising tariffs.

Or, "we might end up with a more muted impact from tariffs than we thought" as tariffs work their way through the economy and suppliers, importers, or others absorb some of the impacts.

Technology may help to blunt the impact.

"We could get more out of productivity, we could grow a little faster, I don't think we need to slow precipitously" to achieve that last mile to the Fed's inflation goal, she said. Daly doesn't want to see the labor market weaken any further.

Right now, she hasn't seen broad concern from businesses about lower rates of immigration.   

Developing... check back for updates.

data.title: Texas Capital Bancshares outlines path to 1.1% ROAA in second half 2025 while cutting expense growth outlook
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Texas Capital Bancshares (TCBI) Q2 2025

Management View

  • Robert C. Holmes, Chairman, President and CEO, highlighted "year-over-year quarterly earnings growth accelerated materially during the quarter with adjusted total revenue increasing 16%, adjusted net income to common up 100%, adjusted earnings per share expanding 104%, and adjusted return on average assets of 1.02%, nearing the 1.1 goal we set out for 2025." He stated that commercial loans grew 5% linked quarter and are up 13% year-over-year. Holmes emphasized treasury product fees, which "increased 37% year-over-year to a record high for the firm." He noted continued investments in technology and platform expansion, including further build-out of the equities platform and ongoing enhancements to research coverage and trading operations.
  • John Matthew Scurlock, CFO, reported "second quarter adjusted total revenue increased $42.3 million or 16% relative to Q2 of last year, supported by 17% growth in net interest income and 11% growth in adjusted fee-based revenue." He said, "Adjusted total noninterest expense decreased $14.1 million quarter-over-quarter." Scurlock further noted, "adjusted net income to common was $75.5 million, an increase of 100% compared to adjusted net income to common in Q2 of last year." He cited ending period cash and securities at 23% of total assets and highlighted that average commercial loan balances increased 4% or $399 million during the quarter.

Outlook

  • The company reaffirmed its full-year revenue guidance of low double-digit percent growth. Scurlock stated, "We are decreasing our noninterest expense outlook to mid- to high single-digit percent growth from high single-digit percent growth previously." The provision expense outlook remains "30 to 35 basis points of loans held for investment, excluding mortgage finance."
  • Holmes said, "achievement of quarterly 1.1% ROAA in the second half of the year" is expected.

Financial Results

  • Adjusted preprovision net revenue increased 52% year-over-year or $41.4 million to $120.5 million, marking a record level since the strategic transformation announcement. The allowance for credit loss increased $2 million to $334 million, or 1.79% of LHI excluding mortgage finance. Ending period gross LHI balances, excluding mortgage finance, grew $387 million or 9% annualized during the quarter.
  • Average mortgage finance loans increased 34% linked quarter to $5.3 billion. Client interest-bearing deposits are up $2.8 billion or 19% year-over-year. Net interest margin expanded 16 basis points this quarter, driven by improvements in funding costs and growth in loan balances. CET1 finished at 11.45%.
  • The company repurchased approximately 318,000 shares for a total of $21 million at a weighted average price of $65.50 per share.

Q&A

  • Michael Edward Rose, Raymond James: Asked about investment banking and trading pipeline trends and the effects of recent hires. Scurlock responded that despite capital markets being "essentially closed in April and through the first part of May, investment banking and trading income did come in above the guide," with expectations for "total noninterest income to move to $60 million to $65 million in the third quarter," supported by investment banking fees. Holmes added that the platform's expansion improved NIM and client relationships, noting a "42 bps improvement year-to-date in NIM."
  • Rose: Inquired about the sustainability of exceeding 1.1% ROA. Holmes said, "That was a guidepost along the way of the transformation, and we have a long way to go, and we're super excited about it."
  • Wood Neblett Lay, Keefe, Bruyette & Woods: Asked about expense guidance and restructuring charges. Scurlock explained, "We continue to find opportunities to drive what we term as real structural efficiencies," and expects noninterest expense to be "mid- to high 190s in the next 2 quarters."
  • Lay: Questioned capital management given regulatory shifts. Holmes replied, "We are super happy to have what you would call excess capital, we call a strategic advantage in the market."
  • Stephen Kendall Scouten, Piper Sandler: Asked what could push revenue to the high end of the guide. Scurlock said, "At $230 million of fees, so the low end of the fee guide, we've got enough NII momentum, Stephen, to move to the high end of the current guide."
  • Matt Olney, Stephens Inc.: Inquired about commercial lending trends and customer behavior. Scurlock said, "Pipeline suggests that those client acquisition trends should remain intact heading into the third quarter."
  • Olney: Asked about mortgage finance guidance amid a softer industry outlook. Scurlock said, "Our expectation for the market hasn't changed since the beginning of the year. So we've got a $1.9 trillion origination market that sits on top of 30-year fixed rate mortgages between 6.8% and 7%."
  • Jon Glenn Arfstrom, RBC Capital Markets: Asked about higher NPL balances. Scurlock cited a "couple of C&I credits, not industry-specific, no direct impact from tariffs."
  • Arfstrom: Asked about wealth management progress. Holmes said, "It is the last leg. We're a little behind on that. We've spoken about it. We went on to the new platform in the fourth quarter of last year."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts focused on sustainability of fee and loan growth, expense management, and capital deployment, with a generally positive but probing tone. Questions reflected interest in the durability of revenue momentum, success of strategic investments, and the impact of macroeconomic factors.
  • Management maintained a confident tone, emphasizing "structurally higher and more sustainable earnings" and expressing certainty in meeting and exceeding the ROAA target. Holmes stated, "What we're certain of is that the strategy works, the client acceptance of this strategy and our bankers is actually surprising even to me."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management's tone was more assertive about achieving performance targets and scaling new business lines. Analyst sentiment remained positive but with more emphasis on near-term execution and structural expense improvements.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Revenue growth accelerated from 9% in Q1 to 16% in Q2, with net interest income and fee-based revenue both higher. Commercial loan growth increased from 10% year-over-year in Q1 to 13% year-over-year in Q2. Treasury product fee growth rose sharply from 22% in Q1 to 37% in Q2. Mortgage finance loans rebounded from a seasonal low in Q1 to a 34% linked quarter increase in Q2.
  • Expense guidance was reduced from high single-digit growth in Q1 to mid- to high single-digit growth in Q2, citing realized structural efficiencies. Management reaffirmed the commitment to a 1.1% ROAA target and indicated ongoing momentum.
  • Analysts in Q2 focused more on structural expense control and the scaling of investment banking, while Q1 questions leaned toward the resilience of fee pipelines and credit quality amid macro uncertainty. Management's confidence and emphasis on platform scalability and strategic execution increased quarter-over-quarter.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management noted a "continued view of the uncertain macroeconomic environment, which remains decidedly more conservative than consensus expectations." The allowance for credit loss was increased to $334 million, and the company continues to proactively assess credit impact across macroeconomic and portfolio-specific scenarios.
  • Analysts probed on potential expense variability, the impact of regulatory capital changes, and the sustainability of fee and loan growth. Management pointed to ongoing enhancements in credit structures and a disciplined capital allocation approach as mitigation strategies.

Final Takeaway

Texas Capital Bancshares delivered robust growth in Q2 2025, with record revenue and earnings, peer-leading capital ratios, and strong momentum in commercial lending, treasury products, and investment banking. Management reaffirmed low double-digit percent growth in revenue, reduced expense growth expectations, and remains confident in reaching a 1.1% ROAA in the second half of the year, supported by diversified earnings streams, operational efficiencies, and disciplined risk management.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Northern Dynasty Minerals plunges in biggest one-day drop since 2020
data.description:
Northern Dynasty Minerals (NYSE:NAK) -28.2% in Thursday's trading, plunging as much as 55% before paring losses, on no apparent news, although three company directors sold a combined 353K shares earlier this week.

The company is in talks with the Trump administration to advance its Pebble project in Alaska, which it says its the world's largest undeveloped copper and gold resource; the asset also has significant resources of silver, molybdenum and rhenium.

The proposed mine near Bristol Bay has been controversial for years, and the Environmental Protection Agency under former President Biden effectively blocked its development of the deposit over concerns it would harm the region's sockeye salmon industry.

Northern Dynasty (NYSE:NAK) shares had more than tripled YTD on optimism that the Trump presidency might roll back regulatory hurdles.
